explanation: Rule 10.10.2 states if the goalkeeper is moved or unbalanced by contact from an opposing player while not in possession, it is an illegal tackle.

question: An attacker pushes a defender into the goalkeeper, and no defender has the ball. Who is penalised?
options: The defender
The goalkeeper
The attacker
No one
explanation: Rule 10.10.3 states if an attacker moves the goalkeeper by pushing a defender into them, and no defender has possession, the attacker is penalised.

question: A defender is pushed towards the goalkeeper but has an opportunity to avoid contact. If they don’t avoid it, who is penalised?
options: The attacker who pushed
The defender who didn’t avoid it
The goalkeeper
The attacker is not penalised
explanation: Rule 10.10.4 states if the defender has an opportunity to avoid contact with the goalkeeper after being pushed but does not, the attacker will not be penalised.

question: A defender pushes an attacker onto the goalkeeper. Is the attacker penalised?
options: Yes, always
Yes, if the goalkeeper is moved
No, the attacker is not penalised
Only if the attacker had the ball
explanation: Rule 10.10.5 states if a defender pushes the attacker onto the goalkeeper, the attacker will not be penalised.

question: An attacker is pushed by a defender towards the goalkeeper but has opportunity to avoid contact. What happens if the attacker does not avoid it?
options: The defender is penalised
The attacker is penalised
No foul is called
A Referee’s ball is given
explanation: Rule 10.10.6 states if the attacker has an opportunity to avoid goalkeeper contact after being pushed but does not, the attacker will be penalised.

question: An attacker with the ball whose original direction would not have led to goalkeeper contact is pushed onto the goalkeeper by a defender. Is the attacker penalised?
options: Yes, for illegal tackle
Yes, for unsporting behaviour
No, the attacker is not penalised
Only if a goal was scored
explanation: Rule 10.10.7 states if the attacker’s original direction or speed would not have led to contact with the goalkeeper, and a defender pushed them, the attacker is not penalised.

question: Within the six-metre area, may a defender push an attacker with their kayak to take the goalkeeper position?
options: No, never
Yes, without penalty unless dangerous play is used
Yes, but only with a hand push
Only during time-out
explanation: Rule 10.10.9 states within the six-metre area, a defender may push an attacker with their kayak to take the goalkeeper position without penalty, unless dangerous play is used.

question: Within the six-metre area, may an attacker actively prevent a defender from becoming goalkeeper?
options: Yes, it is always permitted
No, this is not allowed
Only if the attacker has the ball
Only during a power play
explanation: Rule 10.10.9 states within the six-metre area, an attacker must not actively prevent a defender from taking the goalkeeper position.

question: How far from the centre of the goal line must the goalkeeper attempt to maintain their position?
options: Within two metres
Within one metre
Directly under the centre
Within the six-metre area
explanation: Rule 10.10.1 states the goalkeeper must be attempting to maintain a position within one metre of the centre of the goal line.

question: After an attacker loses possession of the ball, what must they not do regarding the goalkeeper?
options: Touch the goalkeeper’s kayak
Actively impede the goalkeeper’s attempt to regain or maintain their position
Enter the six-metre area
Use their paddle near the goal
explanation: Rule 10.10.8 states after the attacker loses possession, they must not actively impede the goalkeeper’s attempt to regain or maintain their position.

question: During a Referee’s ball situation, when does illegal holding apply?
options: Whenever players touch each other
Only if either player uses the opposition for support
If the ball is between the players
Never during a Referee’s ball
explanation: Rule 10.11.2 states if initial contact is made directly with the ball, illegal holding only applies if either player uses the opposition for support.

question: The Referee stops play (not during a break) due to faulty goals and cannot determine who had possession. How is play restarted?
options: Free throw to the home team
A Referee’s ball
A centre restart
Free throw to the team nearest the ball
explanation: Rule 10.11.3 states if the Referee needs to stop the game where neither team is at fault and cannot determine possession, a Referee’s ball restarts play.

question: How far must all other players be from the two players taking a Referee’s ball?
options: One metre
Two metres
Three metres
Five metres
explanation: Rule 10.11.6 states all other players must be at least three metres away from the point between the two participating players.

question: During a Referee’s ball, when may the players play the ball?
options: As soon as the Referee throws it
As soon as it touches the water
After the Referee blows the whistle
After counting to three
explanation: Rule 10.11.7 states players must make an attempt for the ball with their hands as soon as it touches the water. They must not play it before it hits the water.

question: At a Referee’s ball, must both players attempt for the ball?
options: No, only one needs to
Yes, both must make an attempt with their hands
Only the player nearest their own goal
It is optional
explanation: Rule 10.11.7 states both players must make an attempt for the ball with their hands as soon as it touches the water.

question: Where is a Referee’s ball normally taken?
options: At the centre of the field
At the nearest point on the sideline to the incident
Where the incident occurred
At the six-metre line
explanation: Rule 10.11.4 states a Referee’s ball is taken at the nearest point on the sideline to the incident.

question: While playing advantage, when must the Referee call back the original infringement?
options: After five seconds automatically
If the next pass or shot is affected by the foul or there is no clear advantage
Only if the other team complains
Never, advantage always continues
explanation: Rule 10.12.3 states if the next pass or shot is affected by the original foul or there is no clear advantage, the original infringement must be called.

question: How must referees signal while playing advantage?
options: By blowing a short whistle
By calling ‘play on’ and signalling throughout up to five seconds
By raising a flag
By not signalling at all
explanation: Rule 10.12.1 states referees must call ‘play on’ and signal throughout the time they are playing advantage, up to a maximum of five seconds.

question: How must a capsized player who wishes to re-join the game do so?
options: By re-entering from any point on the sideline
According to the rules of entry to the field of play
By waiting until the next time-out
By getting permission from the Referee
explanation: Rule 10.13.2 states a capsized player must re-join according to the rules of entry to the field of play.

question: A player capsizes and leaves their kayak. May they touch the ball?
options: Yes, they can still play the ball with their hands
No, they may not take any further part in play and must leave immediately
Yes, but only to pass it to a teammate
Only if they are within arm’s reach
explanation: Rule 10.13.1 states a capsized player who leaves their kayak may not take any further part in play and must leave the playing area immediately with all equipment.

question: What is the maximum number of players from one team allowed on the playing area?
options: Four
Five
Six
Eight
explanation: Rule 10.14.1 states no more than the legally allowed number of players (five) may be on the playing area at any one time.

question: Where must substitutes wait during a game?
options: Behind the sideline
In their own substitute’s area
At the centre line
In the spectator area
explanation: Rule 10.14.2 states substitutes must wait in their own substitute’s area.

question: A capsized player exits the playing area at the sideline (not their goal line). When can they be substituted?
options: Immediately
At the next break in play
After two minutes
They cannot be substituted
explanation: Rule 10.14.4 states a capsized player who leaves anywhere other than at their own goal line can only be substituted at the next break in play.

question: Before a capsized player who exited at the sideline can be substituted, what must happen?
options: The Referee must give permission
All of the capsized player’s equipment must be removed from the playing area
A time-out must be called
The team captain must request it
explanation: Rule 10.14.4 states all of the capsized player’s equipment must be removed from the playing area before substitution is allowed.

question: Where must a player collect equipment being exchanged during a game?
options: From anywhere along the sideline
From their substitute’s area
From the official’s table
From behind their own goal line
explanation: Rule 10.14.5 states the player must collect equipment being exchanged from their substitute’s area.

question: Along which line must exit and entry for substitution occur?
options: The sideline
The centre line
The team’s own goal line
Any boundary line
explanation: Rule 10.14.3 states exit and entry for substitution may be anywhere along the team’s own goal line.

question: When must all team members leave the playing area after their game?
options: Within five minutes
Immediately upon completion
After the next game begins
When instructed by the Referee
explanation: Rule 10.16.3 states all members and officials must leave the playing, substitute and officials areas immediately upon completion of their game.

question: What must teams ensure about their equipment after a game?
options: It is locked away
It is removed from the playing, substitute and officials areas
It is inspected by the Scrutineer
It is handed to the officials
explanation: Rule 10.16.4 states teams must ensure all their equipment is removed from these areas.

question: For a goal to count at the end of a period, when must the ball have left the player’s hand?
options: Before the Referee’s final whistle
Prior to the timekeeper’s signal sounding
Within five seconds of the signal
Before the ball crosses the goal line
explanation: Rule 10.16.1 states for a goal to be scored, the ball must have left the player’s hand prior to the timekeeper’s signal sounding.

question: Which signals apply for illegal use of paddle?
options: Signals 10 and 15
Signals 12 and 15
Signals 9 and 15
Signals 11 and 14
explanation: Rule 10.19.1 states Signals 12 and 15 apply for illegal use of paddle.

question: Which of the following is an illegal use of paddle?
options: Blocking a shot with the paddle blade flat on the water
Contacting an opponent’s person with the paddle
Using the paddle to splash water
Holding the paddle above shoulder height
explanation: Rule 10.19.2 defines contacting an opponent’s person as illegal use of paddle.

question: Is it legal to play the ball with a paddle across the bow of an opponent’s kayak within arm’s reach?
options: Yes, always
No, it is illegal use of paddle
Only if the opponent has the ball
Only if the goalkeeper does it
explanation: Rule 10.19.4 defines playing or attempting to play the ball with a paddle across the bow of an opponent’s kayak within arm’s reach as illegal.

question: Using your paddle to restrict an opponent who is using their paddle is an example of what offence?
options: Illegal holding
Illegal use of paddle
Illegal obstruction
Unsporting behaviour
explanation: Rule 10.19.6 defines using a paddle to restrict an opponent’s paddle as illegal use of paddle.

question: Playing an opponent’s paddle instead of the ball is classified as what?
options: Illegal holding
Illegal kayak tackle
Illegal use of paddle
Unsporting behaviour
explanation: Rule 10.19.7 defines playing an opponent’s paddle instead of the ball as illegal use of paddle.

question: Which catch-all rule covers any paddle use that endangers a player?
options: 10.19.2 — contacting opponent’s person
10.19.9 — any other use of paddle that endangers a player
10.26.2 — unsporting behaviour
10.22.3 — illegal kayak tackle
explanation: Rule 10.19.9 covers any other use of a paddle that endangers a player.

question: Under what condition may a goalkeeper place their paddle within arm’s reach of an opponent who has the ball?
options: Never, goalkeepers follow the same rules
When directly defending a shot, provided the paddle is not moved towards the opponent and no significant contact results
Only during a goal penalty shot
Only within the six-metre area
explanation: Rule 10.19.5 states a goalkeeper may defend a shot provided the paddle is not moved towards the opponent at the time of the shot and it does not result in significant contact.

question: When is a capsized player considered to have lost possession?
options: Whenever they capsize
When their whole body and head go under water and the ball is not in their hand
When any part of their body is underwater
When the Referee blows the whistle
explanation: Rule 10.20.4 states a player who capsizes to the point of their whole body and head going under water is considered to have lost possession if the ball is not in their hand(s).

question: May a player manoeuvre their kayak with their hands while the ball is on their spray deck?
options: Yes, freely
No, this is illegal possession
Only to move towards their own goal
Only during time-out
explanation: Rule 10.20.5 states a player must not manoeuvre their kayak with their hands or paddle while the ball is resting on their spray deck.

question: May a player actively paddle with two hands on the paddle while carrying the ball?
options: Yes, as long as they throw within five seconds
No, this is illegal possession
Only if moving towards their own half
Only the goalkeeper may do this
explanation: Rule 10.20.6 states a player must not actively paddle or manoeuvre their kayak with two hands on the paddle while carrying the ball.

question: When is a player considered to be in possession of the ball when it is on the water?
options: Only when holding it in their hand
When they can reach the ball with their hand and it is on the water, not in the air
When they are within three metres of the ball
When the Referee indicates possession
explanation: Rule 10.20.1 states a player is in possession when they have the ball in their hand or are in a position to reach it with their hand, the ball being on the water and not in the air.

question: What is the minimum distance the ball must travel horizontally when a player disposes of it?
options: Half a metre
One metre
Two metres
Three metres
explanation: Rule 10.20.2 states the ball must travel at least one metre measured horizontally from the point of release.

question: Which signals apply for an illegal kayak tackle?
options: Signals 9 and 15
Signals 10 and 15
Signals 12 and 15
Signals 19 and 15
explanation: Rule 10.22.1 states Signals 10 and 15 apply for illegal kayak tackle.

question: What is the purpose of a kayak tackle?
options: To capsize an opponent
To gain possession of the ball
To slow down an opponent
To defend the goal
explanation: Rule 10.22.2 defines a kayak tackle as manoeuvring a kayak against an opponent’s kayak in an attempt to gain possession of the ball.

question: Under rule 10.22.3.e, how far from the ball must a player be for a kayak tackle on them to be illegal?
options: More than one metre
More than two metres
More than three metres
More than six metres
explanation: Rule 10.22.3.e makes it illegal to tackle an opponent who is not within three metres of the ball.

question: When does contact during a jostle become an illegal kayak tackle?
options: When the player is moved more than two metres
When the contact would be defined as illegal under any section of rule 10.22
When the jostled player falls in the water
When it occurs outside the six-metre area
explanation: Rule 10.23.3 states a jostle becomes illegal when the contact would be defined as an illegal kayak tackle under any section of rule 10.22.

question: A jostle can only occur in which area of the field?
options: Anywhere on the field
Between the six-metre line and the goal line at the attacking end
Within the six-metre area only
Along the sidelines
explanation: Rule 10.23.1 defines a jostle as occurring between the six-metre line and the goal line, at the attacking end of the field.

question: Which signals apply for illegal obstruction?
options: Signals 10 and 15
Signals 12 and 15
Signals 9 and 15
Signals 19 and 15
explanation: Rule 10.24.1 states Signals 9 and 15 apply for illegal obstruction.

question: Which signals apply for illegal holding?
options: Signals 10 and 15
Signals 19 and 15
Signals 12 and 15
Signals 9 and 15
explanation: Rule 10.25.1 states Signals 19 and 15 apply for illegal holding.

question: Is it legal to use the goal supports for propulsion or support?
options: Yes, they are part of the playing area
No, using playing area equipment for propulsion or support is illegal holding
Only the goalkeeper may do this
Only during a goal penalty shot
explanation: Rule 10.25.2.b states using for propulsion or support, or moving any playing area equipment (e.g. goal supports) is illegal holding.

question: A player uses their paddle to lift an opponent’s kayak while jostling in the six-metre area. What offence is this?
options: Illegal kayak tackle
Illegal holding
Illegal use of paddle
Illegal jostle
explanation: Rule 10.25.2.c defines using a paddle to lift, pull or hold an opponent’s kayak while jostling in the six-metre area as illegal holding.

question: Which signals apply for unsporting behaviour?
options: Signals 12 and 15
Signals 9 and 15
Signals 17 and 18 with appropriate sanction card
Signal 16
explanation: Rule 10.26.1 states Signal 17 and 18 with appropriate sanction card applies for unsporting behaviour.

question: Which of the following is classified as unsporting behaviour?
options: A hard but legal kayak tackle
Bouncing the ball out of play off an opponent’s kayak to gain advantage
A legal hand tackle to the back
Calling for the ball during play
explanation: Rule 10.26.2.i defines bouncing the ball out of play off an opponent’s kayak to gain advantage as unsporting behaviour.

question: Any infringement committed during a break in play is classified as what?
options: Illegal play
Unsporting behaviour
Dishonourable play
A technical foul
explanation: Rule 10.26.2.a states any infringement committed by a player during a break in play is unsporting behaviour.

question: Is pretending or feigning injury considered unsporting behaviour?
options: No, it is dishonourable play
Yes, it is unsporting behaviour
No, it is not penalised
Only if the Referee saw it
explanation: Rule 10.26.2.h includes pretending or feigning injury as unsporting behaviour at the Referee’s discretion.

question: What is the maximum consequence for a team playing by dishonourable means?
options: A free shot to the opposition
A sanction card to the captain
Disqualification from the competition
A verbal warning
explanation: Rule 10.27.2 states the Competition Committee may take whatever action it sees fit and the team may be disqualified from the competition.

question: May a Referee impose multiple different types of sanctions for a single offence?
options: No, only one sanction per offence
Yes, any combination depending on severity and frequency
Only if the Competition Committee agrees
Only for dangerous fouls
explanation: Rule 10.28.1 states the Referee can impose any combination of sanctions depending on severity and/or frequency.

question: Under rule 10.28.3.f, how is ‘control of the ball’ defined?
options: Having the ball in your hand only
Being in possession or being the nearest player within three metres of the ball on the water
Being within six metres of the ball
Any player on the attacking team
explanation: Rule 10.28.3.f states a player has control if in possession or is the nearest player to the ball and within three metres of it on the water.

question: What does ‘significant contact’ mean under the sanctions definitions?
options: Any physical contact
Any hard contact that may result in equipment damage or personal injury
Contact that moves a player more than two metres
Contact that results in a capsize
explanation: Rule 10.28.3.c defines significant contact as any hard contact that may result in equipment damage or personal injury.

question: For a Referee to award a ‘near certain goal’, what standard of certainty is required?
options: The Referee thinks it was likely
The Referee must be certain a goal was the most likely result
Two out of three officials must agree
The ball must have been within two metres of the goal
explanation: Rule 10.28.3.e states the Referee must be certain that a goal was the most likely end result if play had continued.

question: Which signal applies for a goal penalty shot?
options: Signal 15
Signal 16
Signal 17
Signal 14
explanation: Rule 10.29.1 states Signal 16 and time-out applies for a goal penalty shot.

question: Inside the six-metre area, a deliberate foul is committed on a player passing for a near-certain goal. What is awarded?
options: A free shot
A free throw
A goal penalty shot
A sanction card only
explanation: Rule 10.29.3 states inside the six-metre area, a goal penalty shot is awarded for any deliberate or dangerous foul on a player in the act of passing or positioning for a near-certain goal.

question: Inside the six-metre area, a deliberate foul is committed on a player attempting to take a free shot. What is awarded?
options: Another free shot
A goal penalty shot
A free throw
A sanction card only
explanation: Rule 10.29.4 states inside the six-metre area, a goal penalty shot is awarded for a deliberate or dangerous foul on a player attempting to take a free shot.

question: Outside the six-metre area, when is a goal penalty shot awarded for a foul on a player who is passing?
options: Always for any foul on a passer
For any deliberate or dangerous foul while passing or positioning for a near-certain goal with the goal undefended
Never, goal penalty shots are only for shooting fouls
Only when a sanction card is also given
explanation: Rule 10.29.6 states outside the six-metre area, a GPS is awarded for any deliberate or dangerous foul on a player passing or positioning for a near-certain goal while the goal is not defended.

question: Which signal applies for a free shot?
options: Signal 14
Signal 15
Signal 16
Signal 17
explanation: Rule 10.30.1 states Signal 15 applies for a free shot.

question: Which signal applies for a free throw?
options: Signal 14
Signal 15
Signal 16
Signal 17
explanation: Rule 10.31.1 states Signal 14 applies for a free throw.

question: When is a free throw awarded?
options: Only for ball out of play
For any ball out of play, or when a goal penalty shot or free shot has not been awarded
Only when the Referee stops play
For any minor infringement
explanation: Rule 10.31.3 states a free throw is awarded for any ball out of play, or when a goal penalty shot or free shot has not been awarded.

question: What happens if a player takes a direct shot at goal from a free throw?
options: The goal counts
The shot is retaken
The opposition is awarded a free throw (Signals 11 and 14)
A sanction card is given
explanation: Rule 10.31.4 states a free throw cannot be a direct shot at goal; infringement incurs a sanction with the opposition awarded a free throw.

question: Do sanction cards apply to the team or the individual?
options: The team
The individual only
Both team and individual
The team captain
explanation: Rule 10.32.1 states sanction cards relate only to an individual.

question: Can a Referee skip directly to a yellow or red card without issuing a green first?
options: No, cards must follow the green-yellow-red sequence
Yes, for any deliberate foul of major influence to the game
Only for violent conduct
Only with Competition Committee approval
explanation: Rule 10.32.4 states a referee may move straight to the 2nd or 3rd card level for any deliberate foul of major influence.

question: Can a player who receives a Red or Ejection Red Card during a competition be referred for further action?
options: No, the card is the final sanction
Yes, either Referee may refer the player to the Competition Committee
Only the Chief Official may refer them
Only for Ejection Red Cards
explanation: Rule 10.32.5 states either Referee may refer a player receiving a Red or Ejection Red Card to the Competition Committee for further disciplinary action.

question: Can a player sent off with an Ejection Red Card be replaced for the remainder of the game?
options: Yes, after two minutes
No, they cannot be replaced
Yes, at the next break in play
Yes, but only by the captain
explanation: Rule 10.33.1 states a player receiving an Ejection Red Card is sent off for the rest of the game and cannot be replaced.

question: How long is a player excluded for when receiving their third sanction card?
options: One minute
Two minutes
Five minutes
The rest of the game
explanation: Rule 10.33.3 states a player receiving their 3rd sanction card is excluded and cannot be replaced for two minutes.

question: Is the power play timer running during time-outs or between periods?
options: Yes, it always runs
No, timing is suspended during time-outs and between periods
Only during half-time
Only during injury time-outs
explanation: Rule 10.33.5 states timing of the power play is suspended for periods of time-out or between periods of play.

question: Two players from the same team are excluded. The opposition scores. How many excluded players may return?
options: Both
Only the first excluded player (or a non-excluded teammate)
Neither, both must serve full time
The team captain decides
explanation: Rule 10.33.6 states only the first excluded player or a non-excluded teammate can return. The remaining power play must be served in full.

question: What verbal statement must accompany an Ejection Red Card?
options: ‘Red card’
‘You are out’
‘Ejection red’
‘Sent off’
explanation: Rule 10.34.1 states Signal 20 with red card and verbal statement ‘ejection red’ to the player applies.

question: When should an Ejection Red Card be awarded (rather than must)?
options: For any deliberate foul
For a repeated deliberate or dangerous foul of major influence to the game or that injuries/risks injury
For any third card offence
For any foul inside the six-metre area
explanation: Rule 10.34.3 states an Ejection Red Card should be awarded for a repeated deliberate or dangerous foul of major influence or that injures/risked injury.

question: Threatening, abusive or aggressive behaviour towards a referee during or after a game results in what?
options: A green card warning
A yellow card
An Ejection Red Card
Referral to the Competition Committee only
explanation: Rule 10.34.5 states an Ejection Red Card will be awarded for threatening, abusive or aggressive behaviour towards any referee or game official.

question: Which signal applies when awarding sanction cards?
options: Signal 16
Signal 15
Signal 17 with appropriate card
Signal 18
explanation: Rule 10.35.1 states Signal 17 with appropriate card applies.

question: For which type of foul is a sanction card awarded under rule 10.35.2?
options: Any single foul
A repeated deliberate foul or one with significant influence on the game
Only dangerous fouls
Only fouls inside the six-metre area
explanation: Rule 10.35.2 states a sanction card is awarded for a repeated deliberate foul or a deliberate foul with significant influence on the game.

question: A dangerous foul results in significant contact with an opponent’s head. Is a sanction card awarded?
options: No, unless it is deliberate
Yes, a sanction card is awarded for any dangerous foul with significant contact to arm, head or body
Only a verbal warning
Only if the player is injured
explanation: Rule 10.35.3 states a sanction card is awarded for a dangerous foul that results in significant contact with the opponent’s arm, head or body.

question: Is a sanction card automatically awarded when a goal penalty shot is given?
options: No, only if the foul was dangerous
Yes, for any deliberate or dangerous foul that results in a goal penalty shot
Only if the goal penalty shot is missed
Only at the Referee’s discretion
explanation: Rule 10.35.4 states a sanction card will be awarded to the offending player for any deliberate or dangerous foul for which a goal penalty shot is awarded.

question: Repeatedly and continuously disputing Referee decisions results in what sanction?
options: A verbal warning
A free throw to the opposition
A sanction card
An Ejection Red Card
explanation: Rule 10.35.7 states a sanction card will be awarded for repeated and continuous disputing of Referee’s decisions.

question: Foul or abusive language directed at an opponent or official results in what?
options: A verbal warning
A sanction card
An automatic Ejection Red Card
A free shot to the opposition
explanation: Rule 10.35.8 states a sanction card will be awarded for foul or abusive language directed at an opponent or official.

question: Unnecessary verbal communication directed at a Referee results in what?
options: No penalty
A sanction card (unless an Ejection Red Card is given)
An automatic Ejection Red Card
A free throw
explanation: Rule 10.35.9 states a sanction card will be awarded for unnecessary verbal communication directed at a Referee, except where an Ejection Red Card is awarded.

question: May other team officials (not the coach or captain) communicate with the referees?
options: Yes, at any time
No, other team officials must not communicate with the referees
Only at half-time
Only through the team captain
explanation: Rule 10.36.2 states other team officials must not communicate with the referees.

question: May a coach leave the coaches’ area during play?
options: Yes, at any time
No, except where an Ejection Red Card is awarded
Only during time-outs
Only to assist an injured player
explanation: Rule 10.36.3.c states a coach must not leave the coaches’ area during play except where an Ejection Red Card is awarded.

question: When can the one green card for coaches be issued?
options: Only immediately
Only at the next break in play
Either immediately or at the next break in play, at the Referee’s discretion
At half-time only
explanation: Rule 10.36.5 states the green card will be issued either immediately or at the next break in play at the Referee’s discretion.

question: Is the one green card for coaches a warning with exclusion or without?
options: With exclusion for two minutes
Without exclusion
With exclusion for one minute
Depends on severity
explanation: Rule 10.36.6 states the one green card is a warning without exclusion.

question: A coach receives an Ejection Red Card. What must they do immediately?
options: Return to the bench quietly
Leave the competition area and cannot be replaced
Wait in the substitutes’ area
Apologise to the referee
explanation: Rule 10.36.8 states the coach must immediately leave the competition area and cannot be replaced.

question: What happens if a coach refuses to leave the competition area after an Ejection Red Card?
options: The team receives a penalty
The Referees will abandon the game and refer it to the Competition Committee
The police are called
The game continues without consequence
explanation: Rule 10.36.9 states if the individual refuses to leave, the Referees will abandon the game and refer it to the Competition Committee.

question: A team coach receives an Ejection Red Card. What automatic suspension do they receive?
options: No suspension beyond the current game
A one-game suspension in that competition
A three-game suspension
Suspension for the entire competition
explanation: Rule 10.36.10 states a coach receiving an Ejection Red Card automatically receives a one-game suspension.

question: When taking a free throw or free shot, may opponents contact the player or their equipment before the ball is in play?
options: Yes, they can challenge immediately
No, the player must be allowed to take up position without contact until the ball is in play
Only with their kayak, not hands
Only after the ball is held up
explanation: Rule 10.37.2 states no opponent may prevent the player from taking up position or contact them or their equipment until the ball is back in play.

question: From when do the five seconds for a restart throw begin?
options: From when the Referee blows the whistle
From when any member of the team is in position to pick up the ball and take the throw
From when the ball is placed on the water
From when the player picks up the ball
explanation: Rule 10.37.4 states the five seconds apply from when any member of the team is in a position to pick up the ball and take the throw.

question: After an infringement, where is the free shot or throw taken?
options: Always where the infringement occurred
Where the infringement occurred, where the ball was, or where the ball landed — whichever most advantages the team
At the nearest sideline
At the six-metre line
explanation: Rule 10.37.5 states the Referee will choose where the infringement occurred, where the ball was, or where it landed — whichever most advantages the team.

question: Before taking any throw, the player must be in the correct position and what?
options: Moving towards the opposition
Stationary
Facing the Referee
Within the six-metre area
explanation: Rule 10.37.1 states the player taking any throw must be in the correct position and stationary before taking the throw.

question: A goal penalty shot is a shot between how many players?
options: One attacker and the goalkeeper only
Two attackers and the goalkeeper
One attacker and two defenders
The entire team and the goalkeeper
explanation: Rule 10.38.1.b states a Goal Penalty Shot is a shot at goal between one attacking player and one goalkeeper.

question: When does general play resume after a goal penalty shot?
options: Immediately after the whistle
After the shot at goal has been attempted
After a centre restart
After a time-out
explanation: Rule 10.38.2 states general play resumes after the shot at goal has been attempted.

question: Where must all other players be positioned during a goal penalty shot?
options: Behind the centre line
Behind the six-metre line
In the substitutes’ area
At least three metres from the shooter
explanation: Rule 10.38.4 states all other players and equipment must be positioned behind the six-metre line.

question: What happens if a player enters the six-metre area before the goal penalty shot is taken and the shot does not score?
options: Play continues
The penalty is retaken and a sanction card is given
A free throw is given instead
The goal is awarded
explanation: Rule 10.38.4 states entry into the six-metre area before the shot results in the penalty being retaken if not scored, plus a sanction card.

question: Must the player taking a goal penalty shot present the ball before shooting?
options: Yes, they must hold it above shoulder level
No, no presentation is required
Yes, for at least two seconds
Only if the Referee requests it
explanation: Rule 10.38.5 states no presentation of the ball is required for a goal penalty shot.

question: Must the player who committed the foul resulting in a goal penalty shot receive a sanction card?
options: No, only if the foul was dangerous
Yes, a sanction card must be given (or Ejection Red if appropriate)
Only if they have a previous card
At the Referee’s discretion
explanation: Rule 10.38.8 states the person committing the foul that caused the penalty must be given a sanction card.

question: Which of the following qualifies as a shot at goal for shot clock purposes?
options: A shot that is blocked by any defender
A shot that falls short and stays in play
A shot that rebounds off the goal frame
A pass that crosses the goal line
explanation: Rule 17.1.1 lists shots that rebound off the goal frame as qualifying shots at goal.

question: A shot misses the goal and goes out of play. How far either side of the goal must it go out within to count as a shot at goal?
options: Two metres
Three metres
Four metres
Six metres
explanation: Rule 17.1.1 states a shot that misses the goal counts if it goes out of play within four metres either side of the goal.

question: How many seconds does a team have to attempt a shot at goal after gaining possession?
options: 30 seconds
45 seconds
60 seconds
90 seconds
explanation: Rule 17.1.2 states a team must attempt a shot at goal within 60 seconds of gaining possession or control.

question: After January 2027, if a team retains possession after a shot, how long do they have for subsequent shots?
options: 60 seconds again
45 seconds
30 seconds
20 seconds
explanation: Rule 17.1.2.a states after January 2027, subsequent shots after retaining possession must be made within 30 second periods.

question: What is the consequence of failing to attempt a shot within the shot clock period?
options: A free throw to the other team
Possession and a free shot awarded to the other team
A goal penalty shot to the other team
A Referee’s ball
explanation: Rule 17.1.3 states failure results in possession and a free shot being awarded to the other team.

question: Where is the free shot taken when the shot clock expires?
options: At the centre line
Where the ball is at the time of expiry
At the six-metre line
At the nearest sideline
explanation: Rule 17.1.4 states the free shot is taken where the ball is at the time of the shot clock expiring.

question: If the ball is out of play when the shot clock expires, where is the free shot taken?
options: At centre
From the closest point to where the ball went out
Where the ball was last in play
At the goal line
explanation: Rule 17.1.5 states the free shot is taken from the closest point to where the ball went out of play.

question: Who operates the shot clock?
options: The Referee
The timekeeper
A dedicated shot clock operator
The Scorekeeper
explanation: Rule 17.2.1 states the shot clock will be operated by the timekeeper.

question: When does the shot clock stop?
options: Only after a goal
When the main game clock stops (goal, time-out, or ball out of play)
Only when the Referee calls time-out
At the end of each minute
explanation: Rule 17.2.2 states the shot clock stops whenever the main game clock stops.

question: What triggers the shot clock to restart?
options: Only the Referee’s whistle
The Referee’s whistle or when the player holds the ball up to take a throw
When the player touches the ball
Automatically after five seconds
explanation: Rule 17.2.3 states the shot clock restarts when the Referee restarts play with a whistle or when the player holds the ball up.

question: Can the shot clock be stopped independently of the main game clock?
options: No, they are always linked
Yes, it must be able to be stopped independently
Only by the Chief Official
Only during overtime
explanation: Rule 17.2.4 states the shot clock must be able to be stopped independently of the main game clock.

question: In the last minute of each half, what must the shot clock show?
options: The remaining shot clock time
The same time as the main game clock
A countdown from 30 seconds
It must be turned off
explanation: Rule 17.2.5 states in the last minute of each half, the shot clock must show the same as the main game clock.

question: How many shot clocks must be visible to players and spectators?
options: One
Two
Three
Four
explanation: Rule 17.3.1 states two shot clocks must be clearly visible.

question: Where must the shot clocks be positioned?
options: On the official’s table only
Above, below, or beside each goal, or in field corners on the controlling Referee’s side
Behind each goal
Anywhere visible to players
explanation: Rule 17.3.2 states they must be positioned above, below, or beside each goal, or in field corners on the controlling Referee’s side.

question: What audible requirement must the shot clock have?
options: A whistle blast
A distinctive tone clearly heard by all players and officials
The same tone as the game clock
A verbal announcement
explanation: Rule 17.3.3 states the shot clock must have an audible signal of distinctive tone clearly heard by all.

question: Must the shot clock signal tone be different from the main timekeeper’s signal?
options: No, the same is acceptable
Yes, it must be a different tone
Only at international competitions
It is recommended but not required
explanation: Rule 17.3.4 states the tone must be different to the main timekeeper’s signal.

question: At what time does the shot clock signal sound?
options: At 30 seconds
At 45 seconds
At the completion of 60 seconds
At the Referee’s discretion
explanation: Rule 17.3.5 states the signal sounds at the completion of 60 seconds.

question: How do the Referees confirm the change of possession after shot clock expiry?
options: A triple whistle blast
A single blast of the whistle and award a free shot
By raising a flag
By stopping the game clock
explanation: Rule 17.3.6 states the Referees confirm with a single whistle blast and award a free shot.

question: If the shot clock expiry signal has already started sounding, can a subsequently released shot score?
options: Yes, any shot in flight counts
No, the shot must have been taken before the signal starts
Only if it was released within one second
Only during overtime
explanation: Rule 17.4.1 states the shot at goal must have been taken prior to the start of the shot clock expiry signal.

question: The shot clock expires while the ball is in flight towards the goal. Does the Referee stop play?
options: Yes, immediately
No, the ball is allowed to travel to completion
Only if it is clearly off target
Only during the last minute
explanation: Rule 17.4.2 states if the ball is in flight at the time of the signal, it is allowed to travel to completion.

question: The shot clock signal sounds at the exact moment the ball is still in the player’s hand. Is this a valid shot?
options: Yes, simultaneous counts
No, the ball must have left the hand before the signal
Only if the Referee allows it
The shot is retaken
explanation: Rule 17.4.3 states the ball must have left the player’s hand prior to the signal sounding.

question: A change in team possession occurs without a shot at goal. Is the shot clock reset?
options: No, only shots reset it
Yes, any change in possession resets it
Only if the Referee signals it
Only if possession changes twice
explanation: Rule 17.5.1 states the shot clock is reset whenever there is a shot at goal or a change in team possession.

question: A shot rebounds off the goalkeeper’s paddle and the same team regains the ball. Is the clock reset?
options: No, same team kept possession
Yes, any shot that hits the goalkeeper’s paddle resets the clock
Only if the ball crossed the goal line
Only during a power play
explanation: Rule 17.5.2 states if a shot rebounds off a goalkeeper’s paddle, the clock resets even if the same team regains possession.

question: A shot falls short, stays in play, and the same team picks it up. Does the shot clock reset?
options: Yes, any shot attempt resets it
No, the clock does not reset
Only if it travelled more than four metres
Only if it hit the goal frame
explanation: Rule 17.5.3 states the clock does not reset if a shot falls short, remains in play, and the same team regains possession.

question: A team not attempting a shot loses the ball out of bounds but gets the corner throw. Does the shot clock reset?
options: Yes, any out-of-bounds play resets it
No, the clock does not reset
Only if the ball crossed the goal line
Only if the opposition touched it
explanation: Rule 17.5.4 states if a team not shooting loses control out of bounds and regains via sideline or corner throw, the clock is not reset.

question: Two opponents momentarily both have hands on the ball but one team keeps possession. Does the shot clock reset?
options: Yes, any shared possession resets
No, only a clear change of possession resets it
Yes, after three seconds of shared possession
Only if the Referee signals a reset
explanation: Rule 17.5.5 states the clock only resets if there is a clear change of possession to the other team.

question: A player momentarily fumbles the ball and immediately picks it up again. Does the shot clock reset?
options: Yes, any loss of control resets it
No, a very brief loss of control with immediate recovery does not reset the clock
Only if an opponent touched it
Only if it was on the water for more than one second
explanation: Rule 17.5.6 states a momentary loss of control with immediate recovery does not reset the shot clock.

question: A team receives a free shot because the opposing team fouled them. Does the shot clock reset?
options: No, fouls never reset the clock
Yes, the clock resets for a free shot or advantage from an opposing foul
Only for goal penalty shots
Only if a sanction card was given
explanation: Rule 17.5.7 states the clock resets when a team receives a free shot or the Referee plays advantage due to an opposing foul.

question: At a sprint start, one player is clearly first to the ball. What must the other player do?
options: Continue sprinting regardless
Stop immediately
Make the effort to avoid any dangerous kayak tackle or contact
Move to the sideline
explanation: The Sprint Starts clarification states if one player is clearly first, the other must make the effort to avoid any dangerous kayak tackle or contact with the opponent’s body.

question: At a sprint start, a player arrives late and makes contact with the nose of their kayak on the opponent’s body. What is called?
options: No foul — normal play
Illegal jostle
Illegal kayak tackle with a sanction card
Free throw to the opponent
explanation: The Sprint Starts clarification states late arrival with uncontrolled kayak nose contact on the opponent’s body is considered deliberate/dangerous: illegal kayak tackle with a sanction card.

question: A defender is stationary with their paddle beyond arm’s reach. An attacker moves into that space and contacts the paddle while shooting. Who is responsible?
options: The defender, for illegal use of paddle
The attacker — it is the attacker’s responsibility to avoid contact
Both players are penalised
Neither, it is a Referee’s ball
explanation: The Defender’s Paddle clarification states it is the attacker’s responsibility to avoid contact when the defender’s paddle is stationary beyond arm’s reach.

question: For the defender’s paddle rule to protect the defender, what must be true about the defender’s position?
options: They must be within one metre of the goal
Their hand(s) or paddle must be stationary and beyond arm’s reach
They must be the designated goalkeeper
They must be facing the attacker
explanation: The Defender’s Paddle clarification states the defender must be stationary with hand(s) or paddle positioned beyond arm’s reach.

question: Before the throwing action begins, may a defender play the ball from the attacker’s stationary hand?
options: No, never
Yes, provided there is no forceful action, no danger, and contact is only with the ball
Yes, without restrictions
Only the goalkeeper may do this
explanation: The Illegal Hand Tackle clarification states before the throwing action begins, the defender may play the ball provided there is no forceful action, no danger, and contact is only with the ball.

question: At what point does the throwing action begin, after which no contact is permitted?
options: When the player picks up the ball
When the ball is raised behind the attacker’s head
When the player starts their arm swing forward
When the Referee signals play on
explanation: The Illegal Hand Tackle clarification states the throwing action begins when the ball is raised behind the attacker’s head.

question: How long after the scheduled start time does a team have to field five players before the game is forfeited?
options: Two minutes
Three minutes
Five minutes
Ten minutes
explanation: Rule 10.4.4 states if fewer than five players are ready five minutes after the scheduled start time, the game is declared a forfeit.

question: At commencement, what sanction applies if a non-competing player enters the three-metre radius before the ball is touched?
options: Free throw (Signals 1 and 14)
Free shot (Signals 1 and 15)
Start infringement (Signal 1)
Sanction card
explanation: Rule 10.4.8 states infringement incurs a free shot. Signals 1 and 15 apply.

question: What whistle signal does the Referee use when a goal is scored?
options: Triple whistle
One long whistle blast
Two short blasts
No whistle is needed
explanation: Rule 10.6.1 states a long whistle blast is used when a goal is scored (not a triple whistle which is for time-out).

question: Is time-out mandatory after every goal?
options: No, it is at the Referee’s discretion
Yes, time-out must be used after a goal is scored
Only in the second half
Only if a sanction card has been given
explanation: Rule 10.6.5 states time-out must be used after a goal is scored.

question: How long is the live stream time-out, and how are players positioned for the restart?
options: Two minutes; restart from centre
One minute; restart from the approximate same positions as when time-out was called
One minute; restart from centre
Thirty seconds; restart from same positions
explanation: Rule 10.7.1 states the time-out is one minute, and players must be ready to restart from the approximate same position.

question: A defending player’s body has not crossed the centre line back to their half after a goal. May they participate in play?
options: Yes, once the whistle blows
No, they may not take part until their body has crossed the centre line back to their defensive half
Yes, if they are within three metres of the ball
Only the goalkeeper is exempt
explanation: Rule 10.9.2 states no defending player may take part until their body has crossed the centre line back to their defensive half.

question: Must the rest of the attacking team stay behind the centre line for a restart after a goal?
options: No, they can be anywhere
Yes, they must not cross the centre line until the whistle is blown
Only three players need to stay back
They must be in their own half
explanation: Rule 10.9.3 states the rest of the attacking team must not cross the centre line until the whistle is blown.

question: A team gains control of the ball. Can they still have a player defined as goalkeeper?
options: Yes, the goalkeeper remains until replaced
No, a team with control of the ball cannot have a goalkeeper
Only within the six-metre area
Only during a power play
explanation: Rule 10.10.10 states once a team has control, they can no longer have a player defined as goalkeeper.

question: A goalkeeper leaves their position to attempt for the ball on the water but fails. When do they regain goalkeeper status?
options: Immediately when they return under the goal
Not until the attacker has shot or passed the ball
After the next time-out
When the Referee declares them goalkeeper again
explanation: Rule 10.10.8 states the goalkeeper does not regain status until the attacker has shot or passed the ball.

question: How many seconds of shared possession triggers a Referee’s ball?
options: Three seconds
Five seconds
Seven seconds
Ten seconds
explanation: Rule 10.11.1 states players must share possession for five seconds before a Referee’s ball is declared.

question: Where must the two players place their paddles during a Referee’s ball?
options: In their laps
On the water, but not between their kayaks
Above their heads
Behind their kayaks
explanation: Rule 10.11.5 states players must place their paddles on the water, but not between their kayaks.

question: An incident for a Referee’s ball occurs between the six-metre line and goal line. Where is it held?
options: Where the incident occurred
At the nearest six-metre line
At the centre of the field
At the nearest sideline
explanation: Rule 10.11.4 states if the incident occurred between the six-metre and goal lines, the Referee’s ball is held at the nearest six-metre line.

question: Which signals apply when playing advantage?
options: Signals 12 and 15
Signals 13 and 14
Signal 15 only
Signal 17
explanation: Rule 10.12.1 states Signals 13 and 14 apply when playing advantage.

question: When may the Referee give a sanction card for a foul during which advantage was played?
options: Immediately when advantage ends
At the next break in play
Only if the foul was dangerous
Never, advantage cancels the card
explanation: Rule 10.12.2 states the Referee can penalise the player at the next break in play with a sanction card.

question: Must a capsized player who leaves their kayak take their equipment with them when leaving?
options: No, equipment can stay in the playing area
Yes, they must leave with all of their equipment
Only their paddle
Only their kayak
explanation: Rule 10.13.1 states the player must leave the playing area immediately with all of their equipment.

question: Is substitution permitted during time-outs?
options: No, only during play
Yes, substitution is allowed at any time including during time-outs
Only at half-time
Only with the Referee’s permission
explanation: Rule 10.14.3 states substitution is allowed at any time including during time-outs.

question: After a goal penalty shot at the end of a period, when does the Referee signal end of play?
options: Immediately
After the shot is scored, blocked, rebounds away, or goes out of play
After the clock runs out again
After a centre restart
explanation: Rule 10.16.2 states the Referee signals end of play if the shot is scored, blocked by the goalkeeper, rebounds away from the goal frame, or goes out of play.

question: How is the winner determined in overtime?
options: Highest score after five minutes
The team scoring the first goal is the winner
By a penalty shootout
By a coin toss if no goal is scored
explanation: Rule 10.17.1 states the team scoring the first goal is deemed the winner.

question: Is there a change of ends between overtime periods?
options: No, teams stay at the same end
Yes, with a one-minute break between periods
Only if the Referee decides
Only after the first overtime period
explanation: Rule 10.17.2 states there is a one-minute break between periods with a change of ends.

question: Which signals apply for more than the legally allowed players on the field?
options: Signals 10 and 15
Signals 7 and 14
Signals 12 and 15
Signals 19 and 15
explanation: Rule 10.18.1 states Signals 7 and 14 apply for illegal entry.

question: A substitute places their paddle in the playing area to prevent a goal. What card does the offending player receive?
options: Green card
Yellow card
Red card
Ejection Red Card
explanation: Rule 10.18.2 states the offending player is penalised with an Ejection Red Card.

question: A player uses their paddle to play the ball while it is within arm’s reach of an opponent reaching with their hand. Is this legal?
options: Yes, paddle play is always allowed for the ball
No, this is illegal use of paddle
Only if the player is defending
Only within the six-metre area
explanation: Rule 10.19.3 defines playing the ball with a paddle within arm’s reach of an opponent attempting to play with their hand as illegal.

question: Is a player in possession of the ball if it is in the air near them but not in their hand?
options: Yes, if within arm’s reach
No, the ball must be on the water or in their hand for possession
Yes, if within one metre
Only during a goal penalty shot
explanation: Rule 10.20.1 states a player is in possession when the ball is in their hand or within reach on the water — not in the air.

question: Within how many seconds must a player dispose of the ball after gaining possession?
options: Three seconds
Five seconds
Seven seconds
Ten seconds
explanation: Rule 10.20.2 states the player must dispose of the ball within five seconds.

question: A hand tackle from the side pulls back the throwing arm of a player passing the ball. Is this legal?
options: Yes, if the hand is open
No, any hand tackle from the side or behind that strikes or pulls back the throwing arm is illegal
Only if the player hasn’t released the ball
Only if the tackle is from the front
explanation: Rule 10.21.2.d states any hand tackle from the side or behind that strikes or pulls back the throwing arm during passing is illegal.

question: After a kayak tackle when neither player has the ball, how may the players separate?
options: They must wait for the Referee
They may move off each other’s kayak using their hands in a controlled action
They must paddle apart
Only by capsizing
explanation: Rule 10.22.3.b states after a kayak tackle when the ball is no longer possessed, they may move off each other’s kayak using hands in a controlled action.

question: By how many metres must a stationary player’s body be moved by sustained contact before the jostle is illegal?
options: One metre
Two metres
Three metres
Four metres
explanation: Rule 10.23.2 states the jostle is illegal if the player’s body is moved by more than two metres.

question: A player fends off a hand tackle using their forearm. Is this legal?
options: Yes, self-defence is permitted
No, fending off with the hand or forearm is illegal holding
Only if the tackle was illegal
Only within the six-metre area
explanation: Rule 10.25.2.d states fending off an attempted tackle with the hand, forearm, or elbow movement is illegal holding.

question: When does the ‘act of passing or shooting’ begin?
options: When the player picks up the ball
When the player has the ball and is clearly attempting to pass or shoot
When the arm begins moving forward
When the ball is above shoulder height
explanation: Rule 10.28.3.d states the act begins when a player has the ball in their hand or on their paddle and is clearly attempting to pass or shoot.

question: Inside the six-metre area, a deliberate foul is committed on a player in the act of shooting. What is awarded?
options: A free shot
A free throw
A goal penalty shot
A sanction card only
explanation: Rule 10.29.2 states a goal penalty shot is awarded inside the six-metre area for any deliberate or dangerous foul on a player in the act of shooting.

question: Outside the six-metre area, is a goal penalty shot awarded for a foul on a shooter when the goal IS defended?
options: Yes, always
No, only when the goal is not defended
Only for dangerous fouls
At the Referee’s discretion
explanation: Rule 10.29.5 only awards a GPS outside the six-metre area for shooting fouls when the goal is not defended.

question: What happens when a player receives an Ejection Red Card (not the progressive 3rd card)?
options: Excluded for two minutes
Excluded for the rest of the game and suspended for the following game; team cannot replace them
Excluded for the rest of the game but can be replaced after two minutes
Excluded for one period
explanation: Rule 10.32.3.a states an Ejection Red Card means exclusion for the rest of the game, suspension for the following game, and the team cannot replace the player.

question: For how long is a player excluded from the field after their first or second sanction card?
options: One minute
A maximum of two minutes
Five minutes
Until the next goal is scored
explanation: Rule 10.33.2 states the exclusion period is a maximum of two minutes.

question: If the opposition scores during a power play from an Ejection Red Card, does a player return?
options: Yes, one player returns
No, Ejection Red Card power plays are permanent
Yes, after two minutes from the goal
Only if the score is tied
explanation: Rule 10.33.4 states the return only applies for power plays that do NOT involve an Ejection Red Card.

question: A player receives their progressive 3rd card (red) and disputes it. What must be awarded?
options: Another two-minute power play
An Ejection Red Card
Referral to the Competition Committee only
The card is rescinded
explanation: Rule 10.34.2 states an Ejection Red Card must be awarded if a red card (following green and yellow) is disputed.

question: A player receives an Ejection Red Card. Can they play in the next game of that competition?
options: Yes, after serving a two-minute exclusion
No, they automatically receive a one-game suspension
Yes, if the Competition Committee allows it
Only in pool play, not knockout rounds
explanation: Rule 10.34.4 states the player automatically receives a one-game suspension.

question: A player deliberately contacts the kayak of an opponent taking a sideline throw. What is the minimum sanction?
options: A verbal warning
A sanction card
A free shot only
No penalty if it was minor
explanation: Rule 10.35.6 states a sanction card is awarded for any deliberate contact with the kayak of an opponent taking a free shot, corner, sideline or goal line throw.

question: When the ball is out of the playing area, may a coach ask a Referee for clarification?
options: No, only at half or full time
Yes, when the ball is out of the playing area is one of the two permitted times
Only through the team captain
Only if the ball was out for more than five seconds
explanation: Rule 10.36.1 states a coach can ask for clarification at half/full time OR when the ball is out of the playing area.

question: Does the coach’s green card only apply to the specific coach who committed the breach?
options: Yes, only the individual
No, the one green card applies to all coaches and team officials from that team for the game
It applies to the entire team including players
It applies only during that half
explanation: Rule 10.36.4 states the one green card applies to all coaches and team officials from that team for the duration of the game.

question: After a green card has been given to any coach from a team, what is the next card for any coach from that team?
options: A yellow card
A second green card
An Ejection Red Card
A team penalty
explanation: Rule 10.36.7 states an Ejection Red Card will be the next card after a green card warning.

question: During their one-game suspension, where must the ejected coach be?
options: In the spectator area
Outside both the competition area and spectator area, with no communication
In the coaches’ area but silent
In the warm-up area
explanation: Rule 10.36.10 states the individual must stay outside the competition area and spectator area and cannot communicate with players or coaches.

question: How must a player indicate they are about to take a throw?
options: By raising their paddle
By clearly holding the ball stationary above shoulder level
By calling out to the Referee
By positioning at the sideline
explanation: Rule 10.37.1 states the player must clearly hold the ball stationary for a moment above shoulder level.

question: What is the special exception for free shots within two metres of the goal?
options: The ball must travel two metres first
Defenders may block the shot with a stationary paddle or hands after release but before it travels one metre
The shot must be taken within three seconds
No exception applies
explanation: Rule 10.37.3 states defenders may block with a stationary paddle or stationary hand(s) after release for free shots within two metres of the goal.

question: Does dropping or fumbling the ball restart the five-second throw countdown?
options: Yes, it restarts from the fumble
No, dropping or fumbling is not considered, provided the throw is taken within the original five seconds
Yes, but only once
The Referee decides
explanation: Rule 10.37.4 states any dropping or fumbling will not be considered, provided the initial throw is taken within five seconds.

question: What happens if the goalkeeper moves before the goal penalty shot is taken?
options: The goal is awarded
The penalty is retaken
A free shot is awarded instead
A sanction card is given to the goalkeeper
explanation: Rule 10.38.3 states the goalkeeper must remain stationary until after the shot; infringement results in the penalty being retaken.

question: Does the five-second possession rule apply to a goal penalty shot?
options: No, there is no time limit
Yes, the five-second rule applies after the Referee blows the whistle
Only a three-second rule applies
Only during overtime
explanation: Rule 10.38.5 states the five-second rule applies from when the Referee blows the whistle.

question: Under the current rules (before January 2027), is there a reduced shot clock for subsequent shots after retaining possession?
options: Yes, 30 seconds
No, it is always 60 seconds
Yes, 45 seconds
Only during overtime
explanation: Rule 17.1.2 states the current rule is 60 seconds. The 30-second subsequent shot rule only applies after January 2027.

question: When the shot clock expires, which type of restart is awarded to the other team?
options: A free throw
A free shot
A corner throw
A Referee’s ball
explanation: Rule 17.1.3 states a free shot is awarded to the other team.

question: Does the shot clock stop when the ball goes out of play?
options: No, it continues
Yes, it stops whenever the main game clock stops including when the ball is out of play
Only if the Referee signals it
Only on sideline throws
explanation: Rule 17.2.2 states the shot clock stops whenever the main game clock stops, including when the ball is out of play.

question: In the last minute of the first half, must the shot clock display the game clock time?
options: No, only in the second half
Yes, in the last minute of each half
Only during overtime
Only if the shot clock is higher
explanation: Rule 17.2.5 states in the last minute of each half the shot clock must show the main game clock time.
